name: Andes virus hantavirus cluster — MV Hondius travel-linked
summary: Andes hantavirus cluster linked to a South America–Antarctica expedition cruise aboard MV Hondius. 11 reported cases (9 confirmed, 2 probable) and 3 deaths across multiple countries, with multinational case-finding and post-exposure monitoring continuing.
operationalRelevance: Long incubation (up to 6 weeks) means travelers and returning expedition staff can present in jurisdictions far from the source. EMS and emergency departments handling unexplained respiratory failure in returning travelers should consider hantavirus pulmonary syndrome and use airborne precautions during aerosol-generating procedures.
whyItMatters: Andes virus is the only hantavirus with documented person-to-person transmission. The Radboud UMC quarantine event after an incorrect blood-sampling procedure shows healthcare worker exposure is a real operational risk if PPE and specimen handling deviate from protocol.
currentSituation: ECDC, WHO, CDC, and PHAC continue active surveillance and have issued aligned guidance. WHO global risk remains LOW; ECDC risk for the EU/EEA remains VERY LOW. CDC issued HAN 528 for clinical awareness in the United States.
geographyNotes: Source exposure occurred aboard MV Hondius. Confirmed and probable cases have been reported across passengers' home countries; ongoing monitoring spans 11 US states and several EU/EEA member states.

detailSections.title: Clinical profile
detailSections.bodyMarkdown:
Andes virus (ANDV) is a hantavirus that causes Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome (HPS) and is the only hantavirus with documented person-to-person transmission via close contact with blood, saliva, respiratory secretions, urine, or feces (CDC HAN 528; NYC DOH HAN #8).

Incubation is 4–42 days. Most clinical screening protocols ask about exposures within 7–30 days; for hantavirus, screen for the full 6-week window (NETEC Clinical Insights; NYC DOH HAN #8).

Clinical course progresses through three phases: (1) a 3–5 day influenza-like prodrome with fever, myalgia, fatigue, and GI symptoms; (2) a rapid cardiopulmonary phase with non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema, hypoxemia, and cardiogenic shock that can collapse within hours and carries the highest mortality; (3) a convalescent phase beginning with diuresis (CDC Clinical Overview).

The Classic Five laboratory findings — thrombocytopenia, hemoconcentration, left shift without toxic granulation, >10% immunoblasts on smear, and rapid respiratory compromise — strongly suggest HCPS when four or more are present (NETEC Clinical Insights, Dr. Maria Frank).

Case fatality is approximately 38% for HPS overall (CDC HAN 528). Diagnosis is via IgM serology, RT-PCR (viremia persists through illness), or immunohistochemistry. There is no specific antiviral therapy; ribavirin has not demonstrated efficacy in controlled trials. Management is aggressive supportive care: lung-protective ventilation, vasopressors (avoid fluid boluses), and ECMO as a bridge for refractory cardiopulmonary failure (CDC Clinical Overview; NETEC; NYC DOH HAN #8).

Poor prognostic indicators triggering immediate ECMO-capable facility transfer: lactate >4.0 mmol/L, cardiac index <2.2 L/min/m², severe myocardial depression or electromechanical dissociation (NETEC, Dr. Bradfute / Dr. Frank).

Contact the CDC Emergency Operations Center at 770-488-7100 to request diagnostic testing and clinical consultation. NYC providers should call 866-692-3641 (NYC DOH 24-hour line). Verify all contact numbers directly with the issuing authority before operational use.

detailSections.title: PPE & infection control
detailSections.bodyMarkdown:
NYC DOH HAN Advisory #8 recommends airborne infection isolation precautions for ALL contacts with suspected or confirmed Andes virus patients — gown, gloves, eye protection, and N95 respirator or higher — citing the documented person-to-person transmission potential. ECDC's Rapid Risk Assessment minimum for routine patient contact is standard + droplet precautions, escalating to airborne precautions for aerosol-generating procedures. When in doubt, apply the more protective standard.

NETEC PPE specification (Dr. Maria Frank / Jill Morgan, Emory Biocontainment PPE Working Group): "Dry VHF PPE with respiratory protection is sufficient for suspect and confirmed Andes hantavirus patients." Minimum: fluid-resistant AAMI Level 3 or 4 gown or coverall; fit-tested N95 or PAPR for respiratory protection.

Aerosol-generating procedures (AGPs) requiring full airborne PPE: endotracheal intubation/extubation, BVM, CPAP/BiPAP, nebulizers, bronchoscopy, airway suctioning. Minimize AGPs in the field; if required, ensure PPE is in place before initiating.

Donning and doffing sequences should follow CDC Isolation Precautions Appendix A. For VHF-specific EMS donning/doffing (with and without active bleeding/vomiting/diarrhea) see the NETEC EMS Model PPE procedures.

Laboratory specimens with potential to contain hantavirus should be handled using BSL-3 precautions in a biosafety cabinet whenever possible. Centrifugation can aerosolize — use sealed rotors or sealed safety cups. Hantavirus specimens are classified UN2814 (Category A Infectious Substance) for shipping; shippers must be trained and certified. Coordinate testing with your jurisdiction's LRN reference laboratory before specimen collection (NETEC Lab Resources; CDC Hantavirus).

Environmental decontamination (CDC "How to Clean Up After Rodents"): never vacuum or sweep rodent urine, droppings, or nesting materials — these aerosolize the virus. Use 1.5 cups household bleach to 1 gallon water (or 1:9 bleach:water). Soak materials for 5 minutes. For healthcare facility and EMS vehicle decontamination, follow your agency's infection control SOP and consult your local health department.

HCW exposure response: remove and dispose of PPE using proper doffing, perform hand hygiene, report exposure to occupational health and infection control, document all involved personnel, and monitor for symptoms for the full 42-day incubation window. Exposed staff should not provide direct patient care pending risk assessment (CDC; NYC DOH HAN #8).

detailSections.title: EMS operational notes
detailSections.bodyMarkdown:
EMS personnel responding to suspected or confirmed Andes virus patients should follow NYC DOH HAN #8 and CDC HAN 528:

- Apply airborne precautions (N95 or PAPR, gown, gloves, eye protection) before patient contact for all suspected or confirmed cases — not only for AGPs.
- Minimize AGPs in the field; if required (intubation, BVM, CPAP, nebulizer), ensure full AGP PPE is in place before initiation.
- Notify the receiving facility in advance of transport so an airborne infection isolation room (AIIR) can be prepared.
- Document all personnel with direct patient contact for exposure tracking.
- Report any suspected case to the local health department immediately.
- Personnel developing symptoms within 42 days of a potential exposure should report to occupational health and avoid patient care.

Andes virus does not spread by casual contact. Documented transmission requires close sustained contact, exposure to bodily fluids, or AGPs without adequate PPE.

detailSections.title: Genomics & laboratory diagnostics
detailSections.bodyMarkdown:
The causative agent of the 2026 MV Hondius outbreak was confirmed as Andes virus (Orthohantavirus andesense) by genomic sequencing at the National Institute for Communicable Diseases (NICD, South Africa) and virus-specific PCR at Geneva University Hospitals (CDC About Andes Virus; WHO DON599).

Laboratory confirmation criteria (CDC Clinical Overview): IgM detection by ELISA in a single serum specimen, fourfold or greater rise in IgG titer between acute and convalescent sera, RT-PCR detection of hantavirus RNA in blood/urine/tissue, or immunohistochemistry detection of antigen in tissue.

Testing capacity is limited to public health laboratories with hantavirus capability. Contact the LRN reference laboratory in your jurisdiction or the CDC Emergency Operations Center (770-488-7100) for testing coordination, specimen handling, and pre-approval before shipment.

detailSections.title: Protocols & authoritative guidance
detailSections.bodyMarkdown:
Core authoritative guidance for the 2026 Andes virus situation:

- WHO Disease Outbreak News DON599 and DON601 (case-finding and risk assessment updates).
- WHO Rapid Risk Assessment v2 — Andes hantavirus, global.
- CDC HAN 528 — clinical recognition and infection prevention for U.S. clinicians.
- ECDC Rapid Risk Assessment (May 6, 2026) and ongoing ECDC Surveillance Update for EU/EEA cases.
- NYC DOH HAN Advisory #8 — jurisdiction-specific case reporting and airborne precaution requirements.
- PHAC Media Update — Canadian situation summary and clinical guidance for Canadian providers.
- NETEC Clinical Insights and Laboratory Resources — operational guidance for special-pathogen-capable centers.

Reporting: HPS is a nationally notifiable condition in the United States. Report suspected cases promptly to your local or state health department. NYC providers: 866-692-3641. CDC EOC: 770-488-7100. EU/EEA providers should follow national notification pathways.

name: Ebola disease — Bundibugyo virus, Democratic Republic of the Congo
summary: Viral hemorrhagic fever monitoring remains focused on Ebola disease activity in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and neighbouring-region readiness. Verify any current Bundibugyo-specific case counts, dates, and affected health zones directly against the latest WHO Disease Outbreak News item or Africa CDC epidemic intelligence report before publication.
operationalRelevance: Filovirus outbreaks demand strict isolation, source-control PPE, and contact tracing. Healthcare preparedness teams should review travel-history screening, airport and port-of-entry coordination, and laboratory specimen pathways for suspected viral hemorrhagic fever.
whyItMatters: Bundibugyo virus has caused multiple regional outbreaks since 2007 with case-fatality rates around 30–40%. Cross-border movement in eastern DRC, Uganda, and South Sudan elevates the risk of geographic spread; no licensed vaccine specific to Bundibugyo is currently available.
geographyNotes: Regional monitoring focuses on DRC and neighbouring countries where cross-border movement can affect surveillance and preparedness. Health-zone-level claims require a specific current outbreak report.

mapMarkers.label: Ituri Province, DRC — active outbreak zone
Mubende District, Uganda — historical Bundibugyo focus
WHO AFRO, Brazzaville — regional response coordination
Beni — community surveillance & contact tracing hub

name: Measles — United States
summary: U.S. measles monitoring remains active because CDC and PAHO surveillance pages continue to track outbreaks, immunity gaps, and regional elimination concerns. Verify current case totals against the CDC measles cases and outbreaks page before publication.
operationalRelevance: EMS and emergency departments should screen for measles in any febrile patient with rash, particularly in unvaccinated populations and clusters. Airborne precautions are required; one infected person in a waiting room can expose every subsequent patient for two hours. Confirm two-dose MMR status for healthcare staff.
whyItMatters: Measles can spread rapidly in undervaccinated communities and healthcare settings. Continued regional surveillance helps identify immunity gaps and protect elimination status.

mapMarkers.label: Gaines County, Texas — large outbreak cluster
Lea County, New Mexico — linked cluster
Los Angeles County, CA — travel-linked case
Prince George's County, MD — importation case

name: Mpox — clade I, Africa & travel-linked importations
summary: Mpox clade I and travel-linked importation monitoring remain operationally significant for clinical recognition, specimen routing, and laboratory clade differentiation. Verify current country-level trends and any importation details against specific Africa CDC, WHO, or CDC updates before publication.
operationalRelevance: Clinicians should consider mpox in compatible rash illnesses with relevant travel or contact history. Confirm specimen pathways for orthopoxvirus PCR and laboratory clade differentiation; isolate suspected cases per CDC interim guidance.
whyItMatters: Clade I has historically been associated with higher severity than clade IIb. Detection in non-endemic jurisdictions raises importation and clinical-readiness implications.

mapMarkers.label: South Kivu, DRC — clade Ib emergence
Sweden — first European clade Ib case

name: Avian influenza — H5/H5N6 zoonotic signals
summary: Zoonotic influenza monitoring remains focused on H5 activity across animal-health systems and sporadic human-exposure investigations. Treat this as a sustained One Health watch rather than an acute human-to-human emergency; verify any subtype-specific human case details against WHO or CDC event updates.
operationalRelevance: Clinicians evaluating severe respiratory illness in patients with poultry, dairy, or symptomatic-animal exposures should consider avian influenza, contact public health, and use airborne precautions during AGPs. Animal-handler exposures should trigger occupational health follow-up.
whyItMatters: Animal-human interface signals are the leading indicators for pandemic potential. Detection of subtypes outside their usual host range, even at low human-incidence, warrants sustained surveillance.

mapMarkers.label: Texas Panhandle — dairy herd HPAI H5N1 detections

name: Cholera — sub-Saharan Africa, including Republic of Congo
summary: Cholera activity across parts of sub-Saharan Africa remains a preparedness concern where WASH disruption, population movement, and healthcare access constraints can amplify outbreaks. Verify current country-specific case and fatality figures against specific WHO or Africa CDC reports before publication.
operationalRelevance: Humanitarian, public health, and EMS preparedness teams supporting affected regions should plan for WASH-driven case surges, oral rehydration capacity, and cross-border transmission. Travel medicine clinicians should review cholera vaccination recommendations for high-risk travelers.
whyItMatters: Cholera spread is tightly coupled to WASH conditions, climate shocks, and population movement. Cross-border transmission risk requires current country-level verification.

mapMarkers.label: Sudan — Khartoum, ongoing outbreak under conflict

name: Seasonal influenza — Northern Hemisphere late season
summary: Seasonal influenza activity is generally declining through the late Northern Hemisphere season but continues to drive healthcare load and EMS demand in some regions. Reference CDC FluView and ECDC CDTR for week-by-week status.
operationalRelevance: Plan staffing and respiratory triage capacity around weekly surveillance trends. Maintain seasonal vaccination outreach for healthcare workers and high-risk patients.
whyItMatters: Even as activity declines, residual transmission stresses healthcare systems already absorbing RSV, hMPV, and COVID-19 demand.

name: COVID-19 — wastewater & respiratory surveillance
summary: SARS-CoV-2 wastewater concentrations and respiratory surveillance indicators provide regional trend signals through CDC NWSS and WastewaterSCAN. Track movement at the metro and state level rather than headline national averages.
operationalRelevance: Use regional wastewater trends to anticipate ED and EMS respiratory volume shifts. Refresh respiratory-virus communication and triage SOPs when local wastewater concentrations rise.
whyItMatters: Wastewater concentrations provide an early-warning lead time over case reporting in jurisdictions where clinical testing is no longer universal.

name: Norovirus — wastewater & community signal
summary: Norovirus wastewater concentrations remain elevated in parts of the U.S. through late spring. Verify regional trend at WastewaterSCAN and CDC NWSS dashboards.
operationalRelevance: Healthcare and long-term care facilities should maintain enhanced cleaning, outbreak detection, and contact precautions during elevated community signal periods.
whyItMatters: Norovirus drives outbreaks in long-term care, schools, and cruise environments; wastewater trends provide a regional lead indicator.

mapMarkers.label: Twin Cities, MN — norovirus signal increase

name: RSV — wastewater & respiratory surveillance
summary: RSV activity is generally declining through the late Northern Hemisphere season but wastewater signal remains useful for tracking regional persistence and pediatric ED load.
operationalRelevance: Track regional RSV trends for staffing in pediatric ED and EMS systems. Maintain immunization outreach for infants and older adults per CDC recommendations.
whyItMatters: RSV is a leading cause of bronchiolitis in infants and severe respiratory illness in older adults; surveillance lag motivates wastewater integration.

name: Human metapneumovirus — wastewater signal
summary: hMPV wastewater concentrations are tracked through WastewaterSCAN and provide a regional indicator of community circulation; clinical detection is limited where multiplex respiratory panels are not in routine use.
operationalRelevance: Clinicians evaluating severe lower respiratory illness in young children or immunocompromised patients should consider hMPV alongside RSV and influenza.
whyItMatters: hMPV is under-recognized clinically; wastewater integration helps regional preparedness despite limited bedside diagnostics.

name: Lassa fever — West Africa endemic burden
summary: Lassa fever remains a persistent West Africa VHF monitoring priority, especially in Nigeria and surrounding endemic areas. Verify current epidemiological figures against specific Africa CDC, WHO, or national public health reports before publication.
operationalRelevance: Clinicians evaluating febrile illness in returning travelers from West Africa should consider Lassa; coordinate with public health for specimen handling and ribavirin treatment guidance.
whyItMatters: Lassa is a persistent high-burden VHF with seasonal peaks and limited countermeasures; healthcare worker infections continue to be reported.

mapMarkers.label: Irrua Specialist Teaching Hospital — reference center
Sierra Leone — Mastomys natalensis reservoir zone

name: Chikungunya — global outbreak activity
summary: Chikungunya surveillance remains active across parts of the Americas and other mosquito-active regions. Verify current country burden and case totals against specific PAHO or WHO updates before publication.
operationalRelevance: Travel medicine clinicians and EMS systems in mosquito-active regions should plan for chikungunya in symptomatic returning travelers; review vaccination recommendations for at-risk populations.
whyItMatters: Chikungunya causes acute and prolonged arthralgia disproportionately affecting older adults and can stress outpatient capacity in outbreak regions.

mapMarkers.label: Buenos Aires, Argentina — imported and local cases

name: Candida auris — wastewater detections, multi-state
summary: C. auris wastewater and clinical surveillance can complement one another for healthcare preparedness. Treat wastewater detections as situational-awareness signals and verify current state-level details against CDC NWSS, WastewaterSCAN, or local public health reporting before publication.
operationalRelevance: Infection control teams should review C. auris screening, isolation, and environmental cleaning protocols, particularly in long-term acute care hospitals and skilled nursing facilities in affected regions.
whyItMatters: C. auris persists on healthcare surfaces, resists multiple antifungals, and can drive invasive disease in vulnerable patients. Wastewater detection should be interpreted alongside clinical surveillance.

name: New World screwworm — active cases and sterile fly dispersal zone
summary: USDA APHIS and animal-health partners continue New World screwworm preparedness and status monitoring for the United States, Mexico, and Central America. Verify current detections, trade restrictions, and dispersal-zone details against the APHIS current-status page and WOAH WAHIS.
operationalRelevance: Veterinary, agricultural, and border-preparedness teams should maintain detection and response posture. Clinicians at the U.S. southern border should be aware of rare but possible myiasis in travelers and ranch workers.
whyItMatters: Screwworm resurgence has economic and public health implications across the human-animal interface and can disrupt livestock movement and food security.

mapMarkers.label: US southern border, Texas — APHIS surveillance
USDA APHIS HQ, Riverdale MD — national response

name: FIFA World Cup 2026 — mass-gathering preparedness
summary: Mass-gathering preparedness for the 2026 FIFA World Cup should emphasize event-based surveillance, travel-linked importation monitoring, and venue medical planning across host countries. This record is a preparedness signal, not an outbreak assertion.
operationalRelevance: Local EMS, emergency management, and public health teams in host cities should coordinate movement-aware surveillance, syndromic-surveillance integration, and clinical readiness across venues, transit hubs, and accommodations.
whyItMatters: Mass gatherings amplify the introduction and spread of infectious diseases via concentrated mobility. Cross-border coordination is required for effective surveillance and response.
